comments.body: Related: Why jackson is serializing transient member also?
For input/output, serialization/deserialization it's preferable to use DTOs, not entities. Precisely because you may need different data for different scenarios. This has been asked countless times.
The thing is it's global. I may need that reference in other scenarios

answers.body:
You can use @JsonIgnore

If you try this:

@Entity
@Getter
@Setter
@Table(name = "\"user\"")
public class User {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;
    private String name;
    @OneToMany
    private List<EmailData> emailData;
}

Then add @JsonIgnore to the cyclical reference back to User so that it is not serialized

@Entity
@Getter
@Setter
@Table(name = "email_data")
public class EmailData {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;
    @ManyToOne
    @JoinColumn(name = "user_id")
    @JsonIgnore
    private User user;
    private String email;
}

title: Cycle during Jackson serialization
body:
Suppose I have these entities:

import jakarta.persistence.Entity;
import jakarta.persistence.GeneratedValue;
import jakarta.persistence.GenerationType;
import jakarta.persistence.Id;
import jakarta.persistence.OneToMany;
import jakarta.persistence.OneToOne;
import jakarta.persistence.Table;
import lombok.Getter;
import lombok.Setter;

@Entity
@Getter
@Setter
@Table(name = "\"user\"")
public class User {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;
    private String name;
    @OneToMany
    private List<EmailData> emailData;
}

import jakarta.persistence.Column;
import jakarta.persistence.Entity;
import jakarta.persistence.GeneratedValue;
import jakarta.persistence.GenerationType;
import jakarta.persistence.Id;
import jakarta.persistence.ManyToOne;
import jakarta.persistence.Table;
import lombok.Getter;
import lombok.Setter;

@Entity
@Getter
@Setter
@Table(name = "email_data")
public class EmailData {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;
    @ManyToOne
    @JoinColumn(name = "user_id")
    private User user;
    private String email;
}

I also have a PUT endpoint that should return a user DTO. The DTO must contain emails. The DTO is serialized by Jackson into JSONs, as usual.

How do I avoid a cycle between UserDto and EmailData when Jackson attempts to serialize it?

I may create EmailDataDto for that specific purpose (in essence, EmailData without a link to User). Thus, UserDto would have a List<EmailDataDto> instead of List<EmailData>. However, imagine there are dozens of relations like that one. Would I have to create a similar DTO for each one? There must be a better way, I reckon.

Here's what I tried:

import com.example.pixel_user_api.data.entity.EmailData;
import com.example.pixel_user_api.data.entity.PhoneData;
import com.fasterxml.jackson.annotation.JsonManagedReference;
import lombok.Getter;
import lombok.Setter;

import java.util.List;

@Getter
@Setter
public class UserRequestDto {

    private Long id;
    private String name;
    @JsonManagedReference
    private List<EmailData> emailData;
}

// EmailData
    @ManyToOne
    @JoinColumn(name = "user_id")
    @JsonBackReference // added this
    private User user;

Result DTO:

{
  "id": 0,
  "name": "string",
  "emailData": [
    {
      "id": 0,
      "user": {
        "id": 0,
        "name": "string",
        "emailData": [
          "string"
        ],
      },
      "email": "string"
    }
  ]
}

Emails "datas" still have references to users. I expected something like this – no serialization of back references.

{
  "id": 0,
  "name": "string",
  "emailData": [
    {
      "id": 0,
      "email": "string"
    }
  ]
}
